one_line_takeaway: A cross-layer security systematization for wallet-holding autonomous LLM finance agents that contributes a five-dimension threat taxonomy, 12 named cross-layer attack vectors, a maturity-qualified assessment of agent-payment protocols (ERC-8004/AP2/x402/MPP/ACP), and a five-layer defense-in-depth architecture, arguing no single protocol covers the full threat surface.
key_finding: The central result is that autonomous-financial-agent security is fundamentally cross-layer and cannot be covered by any single protocol or point defense. Of the 12 vectors, the paper ranks P2T, T2R, T2T, and S2I as the most immediate deployment risks because they convert public inputs, tools, or dependencies directly into authorized financial actions, while C2E, O2P, and N2C are slower-burn/cumulative and harder to detect; R2I and M2A are the least evidenced and are flagged as early-warning categories. The protocol comparison (Table 4) shows every assessed protocol/interface leaves at least one dimension as N/A -- e.g., AP2, MPP, and OKX APP mark Market Manipulation as N/A -- so none simultaneously addresses LLM-layer compromise, long-horizon manipulation, and compliance. Inter-coder reliability on the released replication rows was substantial-to-near-perfect (kappa 0.833-0.871). A key conceptual finding: per-agent authorization controls cannot stop adversarial herding, because each agent's action is individually in-scope while the aggregate is harmful, so market/regulatory-level circuit breakers are required.
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) that builds a unified security framework for fully autonomous (Level 2-3) LLM agents operating in agentic commerce and finance, where the agent itself controls wallets/payment credentials and executes irreversible transactions without per-transaction human approval. The paper's OWN contributions are five artifacts: (1) a five-dimensional threat taxonomy (D1 agent integrity, D2 transaction authorization, D3 inter-agent trust, D4 market manipulation, D5 regulatory compliance); (2) a set of 12 named cross-layer attack vectors (P2T, T2R, A2M, R2I, T2T, P2K, M2A, C2E, O2P, I2M, N2C, S2I) derived via a structured layer-pair analysis, each with adversary preconditions and layer paths; (3) a security-property assessment of emerging agent-payment protocols (ERC-8004, AP2, OKX APP, x402, MPP, ACP/ERC-8183, plus MCP as an execution interface) from the standpoint of autonomous deployment (Table 4); (4) a five-layer defense-in-depth architecture; and (5) a corpus-grounded synthesis over a systematically curated public corpus. Note on scope separation: the individual attacks it organizes (prompt injection, sandwich/MEV attacks, Prompt Infection self-replication, evaluator collusion) are drawn from cited literature -- the paper's originality is the cross-layer framing, naming, protocol mapping, and defense synthesis, not the discovery of the underlying attacks.
threat_model: Adversaries target an autonomous financial agent that is simultaneously an LLM (prompt-injectable), an on-chain/payment-network actor (holds keys or delegated credentials, faces irreversible settlement), a financial intermediary (regulatory exposure), and a node in a multi-agent economy (susceptible to strategic manipulation). The distinctive premise is that no continuous human oversight exists, so compromise at any layer can be autonomously converted into a signed, irreversible transaction. Adversary capabilities span: injecting instructions into consumed data feeds, on-chain token/ENS metadata, transaction memos, or inter-agent messages; compromising or spoofing tools/plugins and MCP servers; poisoning fine-tuning data or persistent memory stores; operating colluding/Sybil agents and malicious evaluator agents in escrow-mediated commerce; front-running (sandwich/MEV) an agent's public mempool transactions; and exploiting KYC/AML ambiguity to structure payments below reporting thresholds. The core thesis is that securing agentic commerce is inherently a CROSS-LAYER problem: threats originate in reasoning/tools/identity/inter-agent interaction but harm lands in custody, settlement, markets, or compliance.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) several vectors (R2I, M2A, C2E) are speculative with little or no released direct-support evidence, and R2I/M2A should be treated as early-warning rather than grounded threats; (2) most analyzed protocols are immature -- ERC-8004 and ERC-8183 are draft EIPs with limited on-chain deployment, AP2 has no widely adopted reference implementation, OKX APP's escrow/dispute features are still 'forthcoming' and inconsistently specified, x402 is not yet standardized -- so many security claims about 'proposed features' are explicitly speculative rather than verified; (3) the released replication artifact is only 30 rows and reliability was computed on the 17 rows where both coders gave non-empty labels; (4) headline scale figures such as '>$3M in agent-to-agent transactions without escrow' are cited from the ERC-8183 authors and are 'unaudited'; (5) no new benchmark, no empirical attack implementation, and no defense evaluation are provided -- the layered defense architecture is a design proposal, not an evaluated system; (6) traditional payment rails and cross-chain deployments are deliberately out of scope. Reviewer caution: care should be taken not to attribute cited demonstrations (e.g., Prompt Infection propagation, existing benchmarks AgentDojo/ASB/TraderBench/CAIA, ACP-Control, ZTRV, Agent-Sentry) to this paper -- they are related work it synthesizes.

datasets_used: Systematically curated public corpus of academic papers, protocol specs, regulatory texts, industry reports, and incident evidence (spanning 1994-2026); PRISMA-style flow: 1,373 database records -> 1,237 after DOI/title dedup -> 37 database-contributed works retained + 105 via backward snowballing/targeted inclusion, with 1,192 database candidates screened out
Released 30-row blinded replication set with per-row source-layer and target-layer labels (used for inter-coder reliability)
No ML training/evaluation datasets are used; this is a systematization/survey paper
quantitative_results: 12 cross-layer attack vectors and 5 threat dimensions define the framework
Inter-coder reliability on the 17 replication rows with non-empty labels from both coders: Cohen's kappa = 0.850 (source-layer), 0.833 (target-layer), 0.871 (joint ordered source-target pair)
23 search phrases across Google Scholar and Web of Science; five research communities (C0-C5 topical buckets)

MEV/sandwich-defense latency guidance synthesized by the paper: private MEV-protected RPC (Flashbots Protect, MEV Blocker) adds ~50-200 ms to next-block inclusion; orderflow auctions (OFAs) add a ~300-800 ms auction window; slippage bounds add zero latency
Regulatory thresholds cited (FinCEN/BSA): Currency Transaction Reports for cash tx > $10,000; Suspicious Activity Reports for suspicious tx >= $5,000 (relevant to the N2C structuring vector)
ERC-8183 was motivated by an (unaudited, cited) figure of over $3M in agent-to-agent transactions on Virtuals/ACP with no escrow or verification
baselines_compared: Not a benchmark paper; no ML baselines. Comparative artifacts are qualitative tables: Table 2 compares agent-integrity defense approaches (input sanitization, instruction hierarchy, output validation, redundant reasoning, formal verification) by coverage and overhead
Table 4 compares security properties of representative agent-commerce protocols/interfaces across the five dimensions: Virtuals/ACP, ERC-8183, ERC-8004, AP2, OKX APP, x402, MPP, and MCP

Positioned against existing domain-general LLM-agent security surveys and against general/finance agent-security benchmarks (AgentDojo, ASB, TraderBench, CAIA) to argue they omit finance-specific attack classes -- these are cited related work, not this paper's own baselines
